items.title: Global Theme Machine: AI and Industrial Themes Extend Their Lead
items.summary: The Theme Model showed significant dispersion, with top-performing themes generating returns in excess of 10%, while laggards declined between 1% and 2%. Top-performing themes were Greening the Home, Cloud Computing, Wearable Technology, AI Enablers, and Manufacturing Onshoring.

items.title: Correlation Corner: Expressions for U.S. Exceptionalism
items.summary: A sharp divergence in economic data, with accelerating U.S. momentum and a weakening G9. likely signals a return to U.S. exceptionalism. This regime would likely support more medium-term U.S. equity strength, despite the triggering of recent tactical risk-off signals, and a stronger USD.

items.title: The High-Grade Beat: The AI Credit Cycle Broadens
items.summary: AI's influence on U.S. investment-grade credit continues to broaden, extending beyond hyperscaler supply into project finance structures and rating action. The first round of IG credit rating actions has gone to the bulls, resolving some of the uncertainty over which credits AI helps.

items.title: Once ECB Hikes Are Underway, What's the Point of a Pause?
items.summary: The ECB is expected to hike rates 25 basis points, with the real focus what may be conveyed about future policy moves. We explain why we're sticking with our baseline expectations for a second hike in July, which is a minority view.

items.title: China Economics: PPI Reflation Is More Than Just an Energy Shock
items.summary: China's inflation readings came in mixed for May. We stick with our forecast for CPI and PPI at 1.0% and 2.8% year over year, respectively, and see labor market conditions as a trigger for more policy adjustments. We explore the details and offer further analysis in this note.

items.title: China Economics: The "Six Networks" Arrive
items.summary: China is accelerating policy implementation to stabilize investment, with the recent launch of the Three Gorges project, a significant AI buildout plan for data centers, and a renewed push for urban renewal. All three fill in details of the “Six Networks” initiative, as we explore herein.

items.title: Macro Trends May Raise Risks for U.S. Advertising
items.summary: U.S. advertising trends have been healthy, with TV stations the only segment to hint at potential weakness. But two macro indicators — a low savings rate and rising gas prices — suggest U.S. ad spending may face some pressures in the second half of this year or in 2027.

items.title: Our Global Athletic Survey Looks Slightly More Positive for Nike
items.summary: Our quarterly global athletic survey points to a weaker macro backdrop across all regions, though promotional levels seemed to have improved this quarter in North America and China, with Europe unchanged. Nike gained or held vs. Adidas across regions; we offer other brand takeaways herein.

items.title: European Auto Manufacturers: The Other Side?
items.summary: European auto makers have been suffering from a combination of severe structural thematic headwinds, sharp EPS downgrades and a complete lack of positive equity narrative in a world increasingly driven by momentum. We look at two ways this underperformance could change and what to watch.

items.title: Global FX Strategy: Our Risk Sentiment Goes From Yellow to Red
items.summary: In the last week our risk sentiment has moved from green to yellow and now to red. While we still see a positive trend for risk assets, multiple catalysts at play would likely lead to risk-off with higher yields; historically, that's a USD-positive backdrop. We consider strategies herein.

items.title: Will the Tech-Led Correction in Japanese Equities Drag On?
items.summary: Japanese high-tech stocks had been driving the market since April but have now seen a significant correction, resulting in sharp declines in both the Nikkei and TOPIX. But we see four reasons to think this tech-led correction will likely be short-lived, with significant upside still ahead.

items.title: Metal Matters: Why This May Not Be the Gold Dip to Buy
items.summary: Gold recently closed below its 200dMA for the first time since September 2023. We lower our 0-3-month point-price target to $4,000 an ounce from $4,300. While we maintain a bullish longer-term view of gold, we explain why this may not be the dip to buy.

items.title: Metal Matters: Why We Remain Bullish Lithium
items.summary: Recent lithium price declines likely reflect increased GFEX stockpiles, improved supply responsiveness, and continued uncertainty around near-term end use demand. But we think the majority of the sell-off is behind us, with prices likely to consolidate in the near term.

items.title: Deep Dive: Unpacking Baidu's AI Cloud Potential
items.summary: Baidu's sustained commitment to AI has produced formidable, vertically integrated full-stack capabilities; we see a pivotal shift that we think is transforming it from a legacy search-dominated business to an AI-centric company. We look at potential avenues for monetization we see ahead.

items.title: Sizing the BoursoBank Opportunity for Societe Generale
items.summary: We expect BoursoBank to be a pillar of Societe Generale’s September investor day. After rapid customer growth in recent years, the next step is to enhance monetization. Looking ahead, we expect greater emphasis on customer service and products to keep growing the customer base.

items.title: Are UK Life Insurers Becoming Global Acquisition Targets?
items.summary: Over the past year, four long-term capital allocators have either finalized or secured long-term partnerships with UK Life insurers. We address key questions about the increasing exposure of AAM in this market, the potential impact on the competitive landscape, and valuation implications.

items.title: Key Takeaways From Our India Investor Conference 2026
items.summary: Our Annual Investor Conference in Mumbai gathered more than 100 companies for track sessions on topics including India's outlook and growth story, AI, geopolitics and more. We offer key takeaways from the conference and thoughts on sector impacts in this note.

items.title: Mongolia Economics: Diversification Gains Face Energy Test
items.summary: Following meetings with officials and analysts in Ulaanbaatar, we remain constructive on Mongolia's near-term macro prospects, forecasting above-consensus 7% GDP growth for 2026. The next developmental priority is to ease strained energy infrastructure, as discussed herein.

items.title: Africa Weekly: Algeria, Angola and Currency Stability
items.summary: Central banks in both Algeria and Angola will probably be able to maintain stability for the dinar and kwanza this year and into 2027, helped in part by oil prices' rise. But the ability to keep both currencies stable over the longer term remains uncertain.

items.title: U.S. Economics Weekly: Looking Beyond Payroll Acceleration
items.summary: Stronger-than-expected May job growth will keep markets and the Fed comfortable labor markets are stable and cautious about upside risks to inflation, but we expect upcoming data to reverse the perceived balance of risks. Our base case is for a 25-basis-point rate cut in September.

items.title: We're Initiating Coverage of Cerebras Systems at Buy
items.summary: We're initiating coverage of Cerebras at Buy. We believe Cerebras has first-mover advantage in the fast inference market and expect it to achieve 40% to 50% market share. Our target price of $340 offers 58% upside from the current stock price over the next 12 to 18 months.

items.title: Our Takeaways From Microsoft Build 2026
items.summary: Microsoft Build 2026 offered an updated roadmap for Microsoft's AI stack roadmap and clarity about its strategic AI plan, including incremental independence efforts beyond OpenAI. Our discussions with developers and integration partners revealed a strong and supportive outlook.

items.title: U.S. Industrials: We See Encouraging Signs
items.summary: We expect AI-related investment proliferation to remain a durable tailwind in the second half and are encouraged by signs of bottoming/improving short-cycle industrial demand that could gain strength as the year progresses. We review our top picks for the near term and beyond.

items.title: China Economics: A Push to Tighten Outbound Investment
items.summary: China has launched a series of actions tightening outbound investments, an effort we see as more about closing regulatory loopholes and strengthening domestic-policy effectiveness than about curbing capital outflows. We expect a limited macro impact, for reasons we explore in this note.

items.title: U.S. Rates Weekly: Rising Rates Distribution, But We Carry On
items.summary: The Treasury market is starting to price a distribution of outcomes for stronger growth and a firmer labor market. This could introduce a non-linear risk of higher yields if inflation also remains high, although we see little risk that upcoming CPI numbers will add fuel to this sell-off.

items.title: U.S. Equity Strategy: Earnings Strength Drives Target Revision
items.summary: We're lifting our base-case year-end target for the S&P 500 to 8100. This is predicated on a big step up in earnings expectations, where we now project $350 and $400 for 2026/2027 index level earnings. Our bull/bear cases imply increasing downside skew risk as the index works higher.

items.title: Reaccelerating Payrolls Now, Rising Unemployment Later?
items.summary: Markets and Fed officials will be more focused on the risk of re-tightening labor markets after 172,000 new jobs in May beat expectations with previous months revised significantly higher. But this  acceleration isn't evident in other data; we still expect softer labor-market data ahead.

items.title: PULSE Monitor: A Double-Edged Sword
items.summary: We see strong payrolls as bad news/good news. The expectation for further Fed rate cuts is pushed out, with risk of a hike increasingly priced in, but a stable employment backdrop supports consumption relative to ongoing concerns about AI job disruption and higher oil/fuel prices.

items.title: Global Equity Strategy: Exuberance Building
items.summary: Global equities are near all-time highs, with signs of growing exuberance. Our Bear Market Checklist is at its frothiest level since the Global Financial Crisis but doesn't yet signal “overexuberance”, remaining below levels seen ahead of past bear markets. We stay constructive for now.

items.title: Global Macro Strategy: Warning Signs
items.summary: We’ve been bullish risk since early April, seeing material escalation in the Middle East as unlikely, and our choice to hide out in AI-adjacent equity longs has worked out well. But with warning signals flashing and much supply looming, we reassess our strategies, as detailed herein.

items.title: Retail Weekend Reading: Quarterly Sales Dollar Changes
items.summary: Earnings season saw some very large retailers put up some big comp numbers: WMT grew total sales by 7%, COST by 12%, TGT by 6%, TJX by 9% and ROST by 21%. That made us want to take a deeper look at where all this share was coming from, a quarter-by-quarter exercise we detail in this note.

items.title: European Diversified Chemicals: Supply Distortions to Protection?
items.summary: Momentum in upstream chemicals appears to be moderating, but we think the potential benefits of EU safeguards against Chinese imports are underappreciated. Our analysis suggests a potential EBITDA uplift of close to 10% across the group. Among other moves, we downgrade Wacker to Sell.

items.title: European Rates Weekly: Right on the Curve
items.summary: The ECB is likely to stay on the curve and perhaps err ahead of it by talking up July; we explore scenarios in this note. We argue that it may be too early to speak about a change in the international valuation paradigm of Bunds, despite well-known structural headwinds.

items.title: Oil Monitor: Our Base Case for the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: Brent oil has bounced up to $98 with hopes for an imminent U.S.-Iran deal fading. Our view skews toward negotiations dragging on, with a base case for a gradual return of Strait of Hormuz flows over the third quarter and low inventories being rebuilt, supporting higher prices for longer.

items.title: We're Adding Glencore to Our European Focus List
items.summary: We're adding coal producer Glencore to our European Focus List. We're bullish on coking and thermal coal, but the factors behind that assessment are uncorrelated, and coal prices have the most asymmetric risk/reward of the major mined commodities from current spot prices.

items.title: We're Downgrading Ecopetrol to Neutral/High Risk
items.summary: We're downgrading Ecopetrol to Neutral/High Risk amid higher oil prices and worries about the prospects for change in Colombian oil & gas policies. On the policy question, the downside risk of the bear case is significant, though we note that risk-reward is still to the upside.

items.title: India Electrical Equipment: The Energy Transition's Missing Link
items.summary: We're initiating coverage of four companies we think will benefit from India's transmission infrastructure buildout: POWERIND, GVTD, CGPI and ENRIN. In this Deep Dive, we explore why we see transmission as the missing link at the center of the energy transition.

items.title: Global Gas and Power Insights: Southeast Asia and Demand
items.summary: Since the start of the Middle East conflict, most Asian countries have seen lower LNG imports on a year-over-year basis. Southeast Asia is the exception; as we explore in this note, it's seen as the demand growth engine of LNG, both now and in the years ahead.

items.title: China Internet: First-Quarter Wrap, Second-Half Outlook
items.summary: China’s internet sector has underperformed in 2026, with a year-to-date return of -13.9%. Looking ahead, we think companies with deep, layered AI capabilities and strong capital will prevail. We review our preferred picks in this note and discuss what we find compelling about those names.

items.title: U.S. Application Software: Back-Office Software Earnings Wrap
items.summary: Back Office Software showed more volatile reactions in the first quarter, with the most positive reactions largely from better-than-feared beat/maintain prints that could be viewed as conservative, while solid beats/raises were more mixed. We offer further analysis and a look ahead herein.

items.title: Thematic Equity Strategy: Rebalancing Baskets
items.summary: We rebalance our fundamental trend baskets, keeping our focus on companies with fundamental momentum vis-à-vis our Positive vs. Negative ROE Trend and Beat-and-Raise+ vs. Benchmark relative performance trades. We continue to focus on visible fundamental improvement for alpha opportunities.

items.title: Asia Pacific Radar Screen: June Update
items.summary: Radar in S&P BMI APxJ made small gains in May from a Momentum outperformance driven mostly by Tech's strong rally. But that rally also drove down Value, which underweighted the sector. The model now favors Korea, Taiwan and Hong Kong; sector-wise, it’s overweight on Financials and Tech.

items.title: Macro-to-Micro: Nuclear Energy Remains in Favor
items.summary: We continue to see increasing appetite for and interest in nuclear energy, supporting our long-term bullish view on uranium demand. But utilities continue to wait for someone else to take on cost overrun risks for building new-generation reactors. We offer more takeaways in this note.
